brief: GAP/WHY: Pneumopericardium is a rare complication of epicardial catheter ablation performed after dry pericardiocentesis in ventricular arrhythmia (VA) patients; its clinical effects on patients remain unclear/undefined. Aim: evaluate clinical effects of intraprocedural pneumopericardium during epicardial ablation in VA patients. DESIGN/SETTING: Single-center observational cohort study; West China Hospital of Sichuan University; enrollment Aug 2012 to Jan 2022; all procedures under local anesthesia. POPULATION: 86 VA patients undergoing epicardial catheter ablation. EXPOSURE/COMPARISON: Pneumopericardium group vs non-pneumopericardium group (compared for outcomes). KEY RESULTS: - Pneumopericardium occurred in 22 cases, incidence rate 25.6%. - 12 of those (54.55%) reported dyspnea during procedure; average onset 5.4 +/- 3.2 min after pericardiocentesis. - Blood pressure dropped significantly: mean BP fell from 119.8/73.2 mmHg to 103.5/64.9 mmHg (p < 0.001). - No case progressed to tension pneumopericardium. - Follow-up median 411 days. - MACE (composite: all-cause death, rehospitalization for heart failure, tachyarrhythmia events): 36.4% (n=8) in pneumopericardium group vs 35.5% (n=23) in non-pneumopericardium group. - Kaplan-Meier: no statistically significant difference in MACE incidence between groups (p = 0.28). TAKEAWAY: Pneumopericardium during epicardial ablation is relatively common but, when recognized early and managed properly, unlikely to progress to tension pneumopericardium; its occurrence likely does not significantly affect long-term patient prognosis.
